claim: Quill is a preemptive, priority-based real-time operating system designed for hard real-time embedded deployments where missed deadlines are considered a system failure rather than a performance degradation.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill is a preemptive, priority-based real-time operating system designed for hard real-time embedded deployments where missed deadlines are considered a system failure rather than a performance degradation.

claim: Quill avoids dynamic memory allocation entirely within the kernel itself, requiring all task stacks, queues, and synchronization primitives to be sized and allocated statically at compile time.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill avoids dynamic memory allocation entirely within the kernel itself, requiring all task stacks, queues, and synchronization primitives to be sized and allocated statically at compile time.

claim: A running task is preempted immediately whenever a higher-priority task becomes ready, with the preempted task's context saved for later resumption exactly where it left off.

supporting_evidence.quote: A running task is preempted immediately whenever a higher-priority task becomes ready, with the preempted task's context saved for later resumption exactly where it left off.

claim: This strict, fully preemptive priority model is what allows Quill's worst-case response time to be bounded and proven mathematically, rather than only measured empirically.

supporting_evidence.quote: This strict, fully preemptive priority model is what allows Quill's worst-case response time to be bounded and proven mathematically, rather than only measured empirically.

claim: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task, while an unrelated medium-priority task preempts the low-priority task and delays it further.

supporting_evidence.quote: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority task, while an unrelated medium-priority task preempts the low-priority task and delays it further.

claim: Quill implements priority inheritance: a low-priority task holding a resource a higher-priority task is waiting on temporarily inherits that higher priority until it releases the resource.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill implements priority inheritance: a low-priority task holding a resource a higher-priority task is waiting on temporarily inherits that higher priority until it releases the resource.

claim: Interrupt latency, the time between a hardware interrupt signal and the start of its corresponding handler, is one of the most closely scrutinized metrics for any hard real-time kernel.

supporting_evidence.quote: Interrupt latency, the time between a hardware interrupt signal and the start of its corresponding handler, is one of the most closely scrutinized metrics for any hard real-time kernel.

claim: The kernel is deliberately structured to keep these interrupt-disabled critical sections as short as possible, since every microsecond spent there directly adds to worst-case interrupt latency.

supporting_evidence.quote: The kernel is deliberately structured to keep these interrupt-disabled critical sections as short as possible, since every microsecond spent there directly adds to worst-case interrupt latency.

claim: Every task, queue, semaphore, and timer in a Quill-based application is declared statically, with its memory footprint fixed at compile time rather than allocated dynamically at runtime.

supporting_evidence.quote: Every task, queue, semaphore, and timer in a Quill-based application is declared statically, with its memory footprint fixed at compile time rather than allocated dynamically at runtime.

claim: In exchange, a Quill-based system can never fail due to a runtime allocation exhausting available memory, since all memory is already accounted for before the system ever begins running.

supporting_evidence.quote: In exchange, a Quill-based system can never fail due to a runtime allocation exhausting available memory, since all memory is already accounted for before the system ever begins running.

claim: Some certification profiles additionally require bounded interrupt jitter, the variation in interrupt latency across repeated measurements, rather than only a bounded worst-case latency figure.

supporting_evidence.quote: Some certification profiles additionally require bounded interrupt jitter, the variation in interrupt latency across repeated measurements, rather than only a bounded worst-case latency figure.

claim: Meeting a strict jitter requirement can be considerably harder than meeting a worst-case latency requirement alone, since jitter depends on the consistency of the kernel's behavior across many different system states, not just its single worst case.

supporting_evidence.quote: Meeting a strict jitter requirement can be considerably harder than meeting a worst-case latency requirement alone, since jitter depends on the consistency of the kernel's behavior across many different system states, not just its single worst case.

claim: Quill 1.0 released in 2015 supported only static priority scheduling with no priority inheritance, requiring application developers to avoid priority inversion through careful design alone.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill 1.0 released in 2015 supported only static priority scheduling with no priority inheritance, requiring application developers to avoid priority inversion through careful design alone.

claim: Quill 2.0, released in 2017, added priority inheritance as described in the priority inversion section.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill 2.0, released in 2017, added priority inheritance as described in the priority inversion section.

claim: The current release's minimal kernel configuration occupies 7.8 kilobytes of code space, essentially unchanged from the 1.0 release despite the additional features added since.

supporting_evidence.quote: The current release's minimal kernel configuration occupies 7.8 kilobytes of code space, essentially unchanged from the 1.0 release despite the additional features added since.

claim: RTOS-Cert Level 4 is among the strictest certification profiles Quill supports, specifically targeting hard real-time control loops in safety-critical automotive applications.

supporting_evidence.quote: RTOS-Cert Level 4 is among the strictest certification profiles Quill supports, specifically targeting hard real-time control loops in safety-critical automotive applications.

claim: Level 4 certification requires that task jitter, the variation in a periodic task's actual start time relative to its scheduled period, remain below 50 microseconds across the certified configuration.

supporting_evidence.quote: Level 4 certification requires that task jitter, the variation in a periodic task's actual start time relative to its scheduled period, remain below 50 microseconds across the certified configuration.

claim: Quill's certified configuration for Level 4 disables several optional features, including the round-robin time-slicing mode, specifically because those features introduce timing variability that would otherwise threaten the 50-microsecond bound.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill's certified configuration for Level 4 disables several optional features, including the round-robin time-slicing mode, specifically because those features introduce timing variability that would otherwise threaten the 50-microsecond bound.

claim: Quill's timing documentation cross-references measurements from the Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite, the same suite used by researchers comparing real-time and best-effort scheduling algorithms across domains.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill's timing documentation cross-references measurements from the Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite, the same suite used by researchers comparing real-time and best-effort scheduling algorithms across domains.

claim: As of Quill's most recent published timing report, the latest available Zephyx suite version was 4.2, predating the GPU-scheduling-specific workload profile added in later suite releases.

supporting_evidence.quote: As of Quill's most recent published timing report, the latest available Zephyx suite version was 4.2, predating the GPU-scheduling-specific workload profile added in later suite releases.

claim: Quill's documentation notes that the real-time-systems workloads in Zephyx 4.2 remain the suite's most relevant profile for embedded RTOS comparison, since embedded deployments rarely involve GPU scheduling at all.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill's documentation notes that the real-time-systems workloads in Zephyx 4.2 remain the suite's most relevant profile for embedded RTOS comparison, since embedded deployments rarely involve GPU scheduling at all.

claim: Quill publishes worst-case interrupt latency figures separately for each supported microcontroller family, since clock speed and pipeline depth both affect the figure substantially.

supporting_evidence.quote: Quill publishes worst-case interrupt latency figures separately for each supported microcontroller family, since clock speed and pipeline depth both affect the figure substantially.

claim: These figures assume the kernel's default configuration; a certified configuration for a specific certification profile may report a slightly different figure due to disabled optional features.

supporting_evidence.quote: These figures assume the kernel's default configuration; a certified configuration for a specific certification profile may report a slightly different figure due to disabled optional features.

claim: Teams pursuing certification for a Quill-based product are expected to complete the following checklist before submitting for formal review.

supporting_evidence.quote: Teams pursuing certification for a Quill-based product are expected to complete the following checklist before submitting for formal review.

claim: Skipping step three, representative-state jitter measurement, is the most common reason a certification submission is returned for additional evidence.

supporting_evidence.quote: Skipping step three, representative-state jitter measurement, is the most common reason a certification submission is returned for additional evidence.

claim: Nakagawa and Ito's 優先度継承プロトコルによる割り込み待ち時間の解析 (2011) provides an early priority-inheritance latency analysis that Quill's documentation cites directly.

supporting_evidence.quote: Nakagawa and Ito's 優先度継承プロトコルによる割り込み待ち時間の解析 (2011) provides an early priority-inheritance latency analysis that Quill's documentation cites directly.

claim: Popova's Анализ времени отклика в системах реального времени с наследованием приоритета (2013) formalizes the worst-case response-time bound Quill's certification documentation relies on.

supporting_evidence.quote: Popova's Анализ времени отклика в системах реального времени с наследованием приоритета (2013) formalizes the worst-case response-time bound Quill's certification documentation relies on.

claim: For a task i, the worst-case response time R_i satisfies R_i = C_i + B_i + sum over higher-priority tasks j of ceil(R_i / T_j) * C_j, where C is worst-case execution time, T is period, and B is the maximum blocking time from lower-priority priority-inheritance holders.

supporting_evidence.quote: For a task i, the worst-case response time R_i satisfies R_i = C_i + B_i + sum over higher-priority tasks j of ceil(R_i / T_j) * C_j, where C is worst-case execution time, T is period, and B is the maximum blocking time from lower-priority priority-inheritance holders.

claim: Convergence is guaranteed whenever total utilization across all tasks remains below the standard rate-monotonic schedulability bound for the given number of priority levels.

supporting_evidence.quote: Convergence is guaranteed whenever total utilization across all tasks remains below the standard rate-monotonic schedulability bound for the given number of priority levels.

claim: The first version intended for outside use, released in 2015 as Quill 1.0, generalized this static-allocation approach into a configurable build system, letting application developers declare their own tasks, queues, and semaphores without modifying the kernel's own source.

supporting_evidence.quote: The first version intended for outside use, released in 2015 as Quill 1.0, generalized this static-allocation approach into a configurable build system, letting application developers declare their own tasks, queues, and semaphores without modifying the kernel's own source.

claim: This adoption feedback directly motivated the priority inheritance implementation shipped in the 2017 2.0 release, which the team considers, in retrospect, a feature that should have been part of the very first release rather than added two years later.

supporting_evidence.quote: This adoption feedback directly motivated the priority inheritance implementation shipped in the 2017 2.0 release, which the team considers, in retrospect, a feature that should have been part of the very first release rather than added two years later.

claim: Building that jitter-measurement infrastructure from scratch delayed the first successful certification by roughly eighteen months beyond the team's original internal estimate, a delay the team now cites as the single most underestimated part of the entire certification effort.

supporting_evidence.quote: Building that jitter-measurement infrastructure from scratch delayed the first successful certification by roughly eighteen months beyond the team's original internal estimate, a delay the team now cites as the single most underestimated part of the entire certification effort.

claim: This recap restates Quill's core design philosophy for readers skimming only the overview section.

supporting_evidence.quote: This recap restates Quill's core design philosophy for readers skimming only the overview section.

claim: The scheduling model that enforces this philosophy is described in full in the preemptive scheduling section rather than repeated here.

supporting_evidence.quote: The scheduling model that enforces this philosophy is described in full in the preemptive scheduling section rather than repeated here.
